local_names.hindi: लाल चावल (पका हुआ) (Lāl cāval pakā huā)
local_names.thai: ข้าวแดงหุงสุก (Khao daeng hung suk)
local_names.romanized_local: Beras Merah Masak (Malay); Civappu arici cōṟu (Tamil); Khao daeng hung suk (Thai); Lāl cāval pakā huā (Hindi)

aliases: ข้าวแดงหุงสุก

thai: ข้าวแดงหุงสุก

cuisine: Pan-Asian — South Indian, Sri Lankan, Thai; sold at Indian provision shops, NTUC FairPrice, Cold Storage Singapore
region_of_origin: Multiple origins: Thailand (red cargo rice) and India (Kerala Matta rice / Rosematta / Palakkadan Matta)
food_subgroup: Whole grain red-bran rice — red-pigmented bran layer retained (proanthocyanidins / condensed tannins); includes Thai red cargo rice and Kerala Matta rice (Rosematta)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for red rice (cooked). Closest parent concept is whole grain brown rice (cooked). FoodOn URI assigned null pending a dedicated red rice term.
ontology.foodex2_description: Cooked red bran rice — closest parent match covering red cargo rice (Thai) and Kerala Matta rice (India); FoodEx2 A0GRD is the closest defensible code for cooked whole-grain rice with bran retained.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for red bran rice. A0GRD (cooked rice, whole grain) is used as the closest defensible parent.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for red rice specifically. Null assigned pending review.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of cooked red rice in Singapore is approximately 150g (one rice bowl), providing around 177 kcal, 3.75g protein, 36g carbohydrate, and a glycemic load of approximately 19.8.

nutrient_source.title: Red Rice Cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy FDC ID 168898; cross-referenced with Thailand Institute of Nutrition Mahidol University (INMU) Food Composition Table for Thai red cargo rice
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — SR Legacy; Thailand Institute of Nutrition Mahidol University (INMU)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy FDC 168898 (rice, brown, long-grain, cooked) used as the primary nutrient anchor for macros and micronutrients. Values cross-referenced with INMU Thailand food composition data for Thai red cargo rice. Red rice nutrient profile is closely analogous to brown rice with minor differences in bran pigment composition. Sodium set to 5mg reflecting unsalted cooking. Values represent cooked weight per 100g.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Thai Red Cargo Rice and Kerala Matta Rice — University of Sydney GI Database; peer-reviewed GI values for red cargo rice (GI ~55) and Kerala Matta rice (GI ~54–58)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI 55 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for Thai red cargo rice. Kerala Matta rice GI range 54–58 from peer-reviewed literature is consistent with this value. GI 55 adopted as the central estimate. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (55 × 36g) / 100 = 19.8 for a 150g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (5mg/100g cooked, unsalted); meaningful magnesium (35mg/100g) and potassium (75mg/100g) content
hypertension.guidance: Cooked red rice prepared without added salt contains only ~5mg sodium per 100g and provides magnesium and potassium that support vascular tone; it is appropriate for hypertensive patients when portion size is managed and no salt is added during cooking.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: GI 55 (low-moderate), GL ~13.2 per 100g; meaningful carbohydrate load at typical serving sizes (GL ~19.8 per 150g serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Red rice has a GI of 55 and a glycemic load of approximately 13.2 per 100g — lower than white rice (GI 72) but still a significant carbohydrate source; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100–150g cooked per meal and pair with protein and non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.2g/100g), zero cholesterol, zero trans fat; proanthocyanidins in red bran provide antioxidant activity
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Red rice is very low in saturated fat (0.2g/100g), contains no cholesterol or trans fat, and the proanthocyanidins (condensed tannins) in the red bran layer provide antioxidant properties that may support cardiovascular health; it is a suitable whole-grain staple for patients managing cardiovascular disease risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Proanthocyanidins with mild anti-inflammatory properties; magnesium and B vitamins support nutritional needs in RA
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The proanthocyanidins in red rice bran have antioxidant and mild anti-inflammatory properties; magnesium (~35mg/100g) and B vitamins (thiamine, niacin, B6) support general nutritional adequacy in rheumatoid arthritis patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Red rice (cooked) refers to whole-grain rice varieties that retain their red-pigmented bran layer, rich in proanthocyanidins (condensed tannins). Two main types are sold in Singapore: (1) Thai red cargo rice — a semi-milled or unmilled long-grain variety with reddish-brown bran, available at NTUC FairPrice and Cold Storage; and (2) Kerala Matta rice (Rosematta / Palakkadan Matta) — a short-to-medium grain unmilled red rice that is the traditional staple of Kerala, South India, widely available at Indian provision shops in Little India, Singapore. Both are cooked by boiling in water. The texture of cooked red rice is slightly firmer and chewier than white rice, with a mild nutty flavour. Nutritionally, per 100g cooked: ~118 kcal, 2.5g protein, 1.0g fat (0.2g saturated), 24g carbohydrate, 1.5g dietary fibre, 5mg sodium. Glycemic index is approximately 55 (low-moderate), lower than white rice (GI 72) and slightly higher than brown rice (GI 50). Glycemic load is ~13.2 per 100g and ~19.8 per 150g serving. The red bran proanthocyanidins provide antioxidant activity and mild alpha-glucosidase inhibition, potentially moderating postprandial glucose. Suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free diets. Clinically relevant for Type 2 Diabetes management (moderate GL, portion control required), hypertension (very low sodium, good magnesium and potassium), and cardiovascular disease (low saturated fat, no cholesterol). Culturally appropriate for South Indian, Sri Lankan, and Thai-cuisine contexts in Singapore.
common_user_queries: is red rice healthier than white rice

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC 168898 (brown rice, long-grain, cooked) — the closest approved-source analogue for red rice — cross-referenced with INMU Thailand food composition data for Thai red cargo rice. GI value of 55 is directly supported by the University of Sydney GI Database for Thai red cargo rice. Kerala Matta rice GI (54–58) from peer-reviewed literature is consistent. All values are internally consistent and plausible for a whole-grain cooked rice.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g set to -1: individual fatty acid fractions (MUFA) for cooked red rice are not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 168898 or the INMU Thailand food composition table; detailed fatty acid breakdown is unavailable in these approved sources for this specific variety. polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: individual fatty acid fractions (PUFA) for cooked red rice are not separately reported in USDA SR Legacy FDC 168898 or the INMU Thailand food composition table; detailed fatty acid breakdown is unavailable in these approved sources for this specific variety. water_g set to -1: moisture content for cooked red rice is not separately reported at the required precision in USDA SR Legacy FDC 168898 or the INMU Thailand food composition table for this specific variety. trans_fat_g is 0.0: naturally zero — unprocessed whole grain with no industrial hydrogenation. sugar_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy — cooked whole-grain rice contains negligible free sugars. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — red rice is a plant food containing no cholesterol. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: naturally zero — cooked rice contains negligible vitamin C. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: naturally zero — red rice contains no significant vitamin A (beta-carotene). vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant-based grain food. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0: naturally zero — plant food contains no vitamin B12. Nutrient values represent cooked weight per 100g, unsalted preparation. GI 55 adopted as central estimate from University of Sydney GI Database (Thai red cargo rice); Kerala Matta rice GI range 54–58 is consistent. GL per 150g serving = (55 × 36) / 100 = 19.8. Source adaptation: USDA SR Legacy FDC 168898 (brown rice, long-grain, cooked) used as primary nutrient anchor; red rice is nutritionally analogous to brown rice with minor differences attributable to bran pigment composition.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is 'Red Rice, Cooked' — precise canonical English name. Local names provided in Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, Hindi, and Thai covering the Pan-Asian cuisine context. G6 satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 118.0, protein_g 2.5, fat_g 1.0, carbohydrate_g 24.0 — all present and numeric. nutrient_source cites USDA SR Legacy FDC 168898 cross-referenced with INMU Thailand. Source is approved.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 1.5, sodium_mg 5.0, saturated_fat_g 0.2, sugar_g 0.0 — all present as numeric values. sugar_g 0.0 justified as confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy in notes.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 55, gi_category 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving 19.8 (150g serving), gi_source cites University of Sydney GI Database. All four required GI fields present and sourced from approved GI source.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodEx2 A0GRD used as closest defensible parent for cooked whole-grain red rice with gap note. FoodOn null with honest gap note. SNOMED CT null with gap note. No codes invented.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well over 200 characters (~1,450 characters). Covers food identity, preparation, texture/flavour, cultural context (Singapore retail), clinical relevance (T2D, hypertension, CVD), and key nutrients.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (low concern), Type 2 Diabetes (moderate concern, human_condition_name correctly set), Cardiovascular Disease (low concern). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence.
